text: This is an IMAS educational video of robotic cholecystectomy. Robotic cholecystectomy is done with the help of Da Vinci SI high definition surgical system, commonly known as a surgical robot.

The surgical robot consists of three main parts. First one is the master console on which the surgeon sits and performs the surgery. The second one is the patient cart which contains the robotic arms which are docked to the ports placed in the patient's body and the instruments are carried by the robotic arms with a sterile interface made by the camera arm drapes and the instrument arm drapes. The third component is the vision card in which the assistants can see whatsoever surgery is going on inside the body with the help of a two-dimensional screen.

The robotic cholecystectomy setup involves inserting a camera port two centimeters above the umbilicus, aligning it with the gallbladder in the right hypochondrium. The patient cart approaches from the right shoulder, with robotic arm one positioned in the left hypochondrium below the left costal margin using an 8mm metallic Da Vinci port with a blunt obturator. Robotic arm two is placed in the right umbilical region or right flank, maintaining at least eight centimeters distance from the camera port to prevent arm clashing. A 5mm trocar is inserted in the right flank as the assistant port.

As we have already described the patient cart is coming from the right shoulder of the patient and the vision cart is kept towards the left shoulder of the patient. Once the ports are made, the robotic cart is docked to the ports and then the surgery starts which is done by the surgeon sitting on the master console.

The assistant retracts the gallbladder fundus towards the right diaphragm using the assistant port. The surgeon utilizes an endorhist hook in robotic arm one and fenestrated bipolar forceps in robotic arm two to manipulate the gallbladder.
These endorhist instruments provide seven degrees of freedom which means that they provide two additional degrees of freedom in comparison to laparoscopy.
The robotic hook is utilized as a right-angle dissector, cutting instrument, and dissecting tool, demonstrating its versatility due to its multiple degrees of freedom.

The surgeon completes the anterior and posterior dissection, creating a window behind the cystic duct. The critical view of safety is achieved, revealing the cystic artery, cystic duct, and liver surface.
The surgeon uses endoristed instruments to dissect the gallbladder to achieve the critical view of safety. A hemolog clip applier is then used to clip and divide the cystic duct and artery.
The three-dimensional high definition stereoscopic vision, which is being seen by the surgeon is unparalleled in any other technology applied to medical science.

The surgeon uses routine laparoscopic scissors to divide the cystic duct and artery. The gallbladder is then dissected off with enhanced precision due to the superior three-dimensional vision provided by the robotic system, allowing for detailed visualization of the surgical field.
The gallbladder is placed into an endobag and retrieved through the umbilical trocar. The robot is undocked, and a 5mm scope is inserted through the left hypochondrial trocar for visualization.
